provenance.title: ARTEMIS: A Neuro-Symbolic Framework for Economically Constrained Market Dynamics
affiliations: Department of Electronics and Electrical Engineering, BITS Pilani, Hyderabad Campus
provenance.abstract: Deep learning models in quantitative finance often operate as black boxes, lacking interpretability and no-arbitrage constraints. ARTEMIS combines a Laplace Neural Operator encoder, a neural SDE regularised by physics-informed losses, and a differentiable symbolic bottleneck. Evaluated against 5-6 baselines on 4 datasets, ARTEMIS achieves state-of-the-art directional accuracy on DSLOB (64.96%) and Time-IMM (96.0%).
key_claims: ARTEMIS achieves the highest directional accuracy among 6 baselines on DSLOB (64.96%) and Time-IMM (96.0%), while remaining competitive on point-accuracy metrics (RMSE, RankIC, Weighted R2).

Ablating the Feynman-Kac PDE loss reduces DSLOB directional accuracy from 64.89% to 50.32% (near-random); removing both physics losses (PDE+MPR) collapses it to 41.77% (worse than random), demonstrating the physics-informed regularisers are the primary source of directional signal.

Removing the neural SDE component dramatically improves point accuracy (RMSE 0.2666 -> 0.0224) while only slightly reducing directional accuracy, indicating the SDE trades point-accuracy for directional signal by design.

ARTEMIS underperforms on Optiver (negative RankIC -0.0555, DirAcc 45.82%, below most baselines), attributed to long sequence length (600), a volatility-focused (magnitude, not direction) target, and a limited 7-feature set — explicit boundary conditions for the framework.
All results reported over 5 independent seeds; ARTEMIS's improvement over the best baseline on DSLOB and Time-IMM is stated as statistically significant (p<0.01, Wilcoxon signed-rank test).

architecture.primary_variant: ARTEMIS (Module 1 LNO encoder + Module 2 neural SDE + Module 3 symbolic bottleneck + Module 4 conformal allocation)

modules.name: LaplaceNeuralOperator
modules.input_shape: irregularly sampled {(x_i, t_i)}_{i=1}^N, x_i in R^{d_x}
modules.output_shape: latent function z(t) in R^{d_z}, sampled at M fixed grid points for the SDE solver

parameters.kernel_parameterization: Laplace-domain rational function: kappa_hat(omega) = sum_k A_k / (omega - lambda_k), K learnable poles lambda_k (Re(lambda_k)<0 for stability) and residue matrices A_k
parameters.time_domain_kernel: kappa(t) = sum_k A_k * exp(lambda_k * t), t>=0 (causal)
parameters.bias_function: b(t) = MLP_psi(TimeEmbedding(t)), Fourier time embedding with learnable frequencies
parameters.discretization: left Riemann sum over observed points: z(t) ~= sum_i kappa(t - t_i) x_i * Delta_t_i + b(t)

modules.notes: Number of poles K, latent dimension d_z, and number of Fourier frequencies F are not given numeric values anywhere in the paper. Quadrature error bound stated informally (O(max_i Delta_t_i)) but not derived with constants.

modules.name: NeuralSDELatentDynamics
modules.input_shape: initial latent state z_0 = E(x)(0) in R^{d_z}
modules.output_shape: latent trajectory {z_j}_{j=0}^{M}, z_M in R^{d_z} used for final prediction

parameters.drift_net: 1-hidden-layer MLP: mu_theta(z,t) = W2_mu * tanh(W1_mu*[z; TimeEmbedding(t)] + b1_mu) + b2_mu; hidden width h_mu not specified
parameters.diffusion_net: Cholesky factorization sigma_phi = L_phi * D_phi; L_phi = Tril(MLP_phiL([z;TimeEmbedding(t)])) + I (lower-triangular, unit diagonal); D_phi = diag(Softplus(MLP_phiD([z;TimeEmbedding(t)]))) (positive diagonal) -- guarantees sigma_phi invertible and sigma*sigma^T PSD
parameters.discretization: Euler-Maruyama: z_{j+1} = z_j + mu_theta(z_j,t_j)*Delta_t + sigma_phi(z_j,t_j)*sqrt(Delta_t)*eps_j, eps_j ~ N(0,I_{d_w})
parameters.existence_uniqueness: Assumption 1 (Lipschitz + linear growth on mu_theta, sigma_phi) invoked for strong solution existence per Oksendal (2003); Euler-Maruyama strong convergence order 1/2 per Kloeden & Platen (1992)

modules.notes: Architecture given at the level of layer composition and equations, but hidden-layer widths, d_z, d_w are never given numeric values in the main text or appendix.

modules.name: FeynmanKacPDEResidual
modules.input_shape: collocation points {(z_i,t_i)} sampled from latent trajectories

parameters.pde_residual: R_FK(z_i,t_i) = dV_psi/dt + mu_theta . grad_z V_psi + 0.5*tr(sigma_phi sigma_phi^T grad_z^2 V_psi) - r*V_psi, with r=0 in practice

modules.notes: Appendix A.4.1 contains an unusually candid, unresolved theoretical discussion (the authors explicitly work through why penalizing the raw residual to zero over-constrains lambda=0, i.e. removes any risk premium, and concede the auxiliary-network approach is 'not sufficient for full no-arbitrage, [but] provides a useful regularizer'). This is a self-flagged theoretical gap in the paper itself, not an ArXivist inference -- N_coll (number of collocation points) is never given a numeric value; auxiliary network V_psi's architecture (depth/width) is unspecified.

modules.name: MarketPriceOfRiskPenalty

parameters.market_price_of_risk: lambda(t) = sigma_phi(z(t),t)^{-1} mu_theta(z(t),t), element-wise/matrix inverse per parameterization
parameters.kappa_threshold: paper suggests kappa=2 for daily data (annualized Sharpe ~ 2*sqrt(252) ~= 32) as 'a reasonable choice' in Appendix A.4.2, but does not confirm this is the value actually used in the reported experiments

modules.notes: kappa=2 is presented as an example/reasonable choice in the appendix, not explicitly confirmed as the exact hyperparameter used to produce Table 2/3 results.

modules.name: SymbolicBottleneck
modules.input_shape: raw input window x_input (basis functions applied to raw features, not latent state, per Appendix A.6)

parameters.basis_library: F = {f_1,...,f_K}: moving averages, ratios, differences, variances, and other elementary operations over lags of raw input features
parameters.combination: y_hat_symb = sum_k w_k * f_k(x_input), L1 penalty L_symb = lambda_symb * ||w||_1
parameters.differentiable_selection: optional Gumbel-Softmax relaxation over candidate basis-function parameterizations (e.g. MA lag): p_k = softmax((log(alpha_k)+g_k)/tau), g_k ~ Gumbel(0,1), tau->0 approximates hard selection
parameters.training_procedure: two-phase: Phase 1 pretrain encoder+SDE+prediction head on L_total (no symbolic layer); Phase 2 freeze everything except symbolic layer, train via teacher-student distillation loss L_distill = MSE(y_hat_symb, y_hat_frozen) + L_symb

modules.notes: Size of basis library K, specific set of lags/windows used, and the actual distilled closed-form expressions obtained are never reported anywhere in the paper -- no example distilled formula is shown despite this being marketed as the paper's core interpretability contribution.

modules.name: ConformalAllocationLayer
modules.output_shape: prediction interval [y_hat - q_{1-alpha}, y_hat + q_{1-alpha}]; optionally portfolio weights w*

parameters.standard_conformal: q_{1-alpha} = (1-alpha)(1+1/n)-quantile of calibration residuals r_i = |y_i - y_hat(X_i)|; coverage guarantee P(y_test in C(X_test)) >= 1-alpha under exchangeability
parameters.adaptive_variant: rolling window W_t of most recent W residuals; q_{1-alpha}(t) = (1-alpha)-quantile of W_t (loses strict finite-sample guarantee under non-stationarity per the paper's own admission, citing Gibbs & Candes 2021)
parameters.kelly_portfolio_qp: max_w w^T y_hat - (gamma/2) w^T Sigma_hat w s.t. 1^T w=1, w>=0; Sigma_hat approximated as diagonal with entries q_p^2 from conformal intervals; solved via differentiable convex optimization layer (cvxpylayers-style, citing Agrawal et al. 2019)

modules.notes: Rolling window size W for the adaptive conformal variant, calibration-set size, and target coverage alpha are not numerically specified. It is unclear from the paper whether the portfolio/Kelly-QP layer was actually used to produce any of the reported Table 2/3 metrics, or is a described-but-unused optional capability.

connections.tensor_name: z_0 = E(x)(0), and z_enc_j at grid points (for consistency loss)
z_M (final latent state) -> y_hat = w^T z_M + b
x_input (raw features, NOT latent state, per Appendix A.6.1)
y_hat (frozen teacher signal for distillation, Phase 2)

differences.description: complete ARTEMIS: LNO + SDE + PDE loss + MPR loss + consistency loss (no symbolic/conformal ablated in Table 3)
latent evolution replaced by 'a simple deterministic transformation' (unspecified exact form)
both L_PDE and L_MPR removed, only L_forecast + L_consist remain
entire ARTEMIS architecture replaced by a flat MLP on the flattened input window (no sequential/temporal structure at all)

mathematical_spec.name: Euler-Maruyama discretization
hyperparameters: Delta t (step size, unspecified numeric value)

mathematical_spec.name: Diffusion Cholesky parameterization
mathematical_spec.latex: \sigma_\phi = L_\phi D_\phi,\quad L_\phi = \mathrm{Tril}(\mathrm{MLP}_{\phi L}(\cdot)) + I,\quad D_\phi = \mathrm{diag}(\mathrm{Softplus}(\mathrm{MLP}_{\phi D}(\cdot)))

mathematical_spec.name: Feynman-Kac PDE residual
hyperparameters: r (risk-free rate, set to 0 in practice)

mathematical_spec.name: Market price of risk and MPR loss
hyperparameters: kappa (Sharpe threshold; paper suggests kappa=2 as 'a reasonable choice' but does not confirm it is the value used)

mathematical_spec.name: Symbolic bottleneck combination and L1 penalty
Total composite loss
mathematical_spec.paper_section: Section 4.5 (3-term version), Appendix A.8 Eq. (21) (4-term version including symbolic)

mathematical_spec.name: Conformal prediction interval and coverage guarantee

tensor_semantics.name: lambda_k
tensor_semantics.shape_notation: scalar complex, K of them, Re(lambda_k)<0

lr_schedule.type: ReduceLROnPlateau (factor 0.5 on validation-loss plateau, per baseline training protocol description in Section 5.1; not explicitly re-stated for ARTEMIS itself but implied by 'training protocol' reuse)

training_pipeline.mixed_precision: true (explicitly stated: 'Mixed-precision training is employed to accelerate computation and reduce memory usage', Section 5.1, applied to all models including ARTEMIS)
training_pipeline.curriculum: Two-phase: Phase 1 pretrains encoder+SDE+prediction head jointly under L_total (Algorithm 1, lines 3-26); Phase 2 freezes everything except the symbolic layer and trains it via distillation (Algorithm 1, lines 27-35); conformal calibration is a third, non-gradient post-training step (Algorithm 1, lines 36-38).

datasets.name: Jane Street (anonymised market data, Kaggle competition)
datasets.split: temporal: early dates train (first 500 days), intermediate val, later test; window length 20, 79 features

datasets.name: Optiver Realized Volatility (limit order book, Kaggle competition)
datasets.split: original competition temporal split; window length 600, 7 derived features

datasets.name: Time-IMM EPA-Air (hourly air quality, 8 US counties)
datasets.split: 70/15/15 temporal split per entity; window length 24, 4 features

datasets.name: DSLOB (Deep Synthetic Limit Order Book) -- novel synthetic dataset introduced by this paper
datasets.split: Normal (train) / Stress (val) / Crash (test) regimes; window length 20, 85 features

baselines: XGBoost (mentioned in abstract as a baseline but never appears in Table 2 or Section 5 baseline descriptions -- see ambiguities)
special_conditions: All models trained/evaluated on identical preprocessed windows per dataset for fairness.
Mixed-precision training used for all models including ARTEMIS.
Early stopping on validation loss; checkpoint with lowest validation loss used for test evaluation.

Results averaged over 5 independent random seeds; ARTEMIS vs best-baseline improvement on DSLOB and Time-IMM claimed statistically significant at p<0.01 via Wilcoxon signed-rank test (test details -- which metric, exact baseline compared -- not fully specified).
DSLOB ablation ran for 10 epochs per Figure 6 loss curves.

implementation_assumptions.assumption: Latent dimension d_z, Wiener dimension d_w, number of LNO poles K, MLP hidden widths for drift/diffusion/auxiliary-pricing networks are never given numeric values anywhere in the paper (main text or appendix). Figure 3's caption mentions '64-dimensional latent space', which is the only concrete clue to d_z.
implementation_assumptions.basis: Figure 3 caption is the sole quantitative hint (d_z=64); all other dimensions assumed from typical scientific-ML/neural-SDE literature defaults
alternatives: d_z could be any value; 64 is inferred only from one figure caption, not confirmed in the main architecture description
hidden widths could range widely (64-512) with no textual anchor

implementation_assumptions.assumption: Euler-Maruyama step size Delta t and number of grid points M are not given numeric values. Figure 2's caption mentions a '100-timestep input window', suggesting M=100 for at least the DSLOB crash-regime analysis figures, but this is not confirmed as the training-time M.
implementation_assumptions.basis: Figure 2 caption is the only numeric hint; not confirmed to be the value used during actual training in Algorithm 1
alternatives: M could differ between the analysis figures (Figs 2-3) and the actual training loop
Delta t could be uniform 1/M or dataset-dependent

implementation_assumptions.assumption: Loss weights lambda_1 (PDE), lambda_2 (MPR), lambda_3 (consistency), lambda_4 (symbolic) are described only qualitatively ('we set them so that the economic losses are of the same order of magnitude as the forecast loss during early training') -- no numeric values given.
alternatives: Any lambda_i in a plausible 0.01-10 range depending on loss-term scale at initialization
Adaptive/learned loss weighting (e.g. uncertainty weighting) could have been used instead of fixed constants, though the paper describes fixed weights

implementation_assumptions.assumption: Sharpe threshold kappa for the MPR penalty is suggested as kappa=2 'for daily data' in Appendix A.4.2 as 'a reasonable choice', but this is presented as an illustrative example, not explicitly confirmed as the value used to produce the reported Table 2/3 results (which span 4 datasets at different frequencies: intraday, 10-min windows, hourly, and synthetic).
implementation_assumptions.basis: explicit paper text flags this as an example rather than a confirmed hyperparameter
alternatives: kappa may have been tuned per-dataset given the very different frequencies (intraday Jane Street vs hourly Time-IMM)
kappa=2 could be a global constant used everywhere as suggested

implementation_assumptions.assumption: The Feynman-Kac PDE regularizer's theoretical justification is explicitly incomplete: Appendix A.4.1 contains the authors' own unresolved reasoning trail ('However, since we do not know mu^Q a priori...This is too restrictive...Instead, we note that...A practical way to enforce it is to require that the residual is small for many randomly chosen V. In our implementation, we use a single auxiliary network V_psi...'). The paper explicitly concedes this 'is not sufficient for full no-arbitrage, [but] provides a useful regularizer.'
implementation_assumptions.basis: explicit self-flagged limitation directly quoted (paraphrased) from Appendix A.4.1 of the paper itself
alternatives: A more principled implementation might jointly learn or estimate mu^Q rather than penalizing a single auxiliary V_psi's residual under the physical-measure drift
Multiple auxiliary pricing networks (a 'family of functions V_psi') as the paper itself suggests would be needed for a more complete constraint
implementation_assumptions.affects_section: FeynmanKacPDEResidual -- this is a theoretical/scientific-validity flag, not just an implementation-detail gap

implementation_assumptions.assumption: Basis-function library size K for the symbolic bottleneck, and the actual distilled closed-form expressions obtained from any experiment, are never reported. No example symbolic formula appears anywhere in the paper despite interpretability via 'closed-form, interpretable expressions' being one of the paper's four headline contributions.
implementation_assumptions.basis: absence of any example output from the symbolic bottleneck throughout the entire paper (main text, results tables, appendix)
alternatives: Library likely includes standard technical-analysis-style features (moving averages of several lags, ratios, differences, rolling variance) applied per input channel, per the description in Appendix A.6.1, but exact enumeration and count are unknown

implementation_assumptions.assumption: XGBoost is listed in the abstract as one of 'six strong baselines' but never appears in Table 2 (Master Benchmark Results), never receives a subsection in Section 5 (which describes exactly five baselines: LSTM, Transformer, NS-Transformer, Informer, Chronos-2), and is not mentioned in the ablation study. This is an inconsistency within the paper itself, not an ArXivist inference.
implementation_assumptions.basis: direct textual/tabular inconsistency: abstract says 'six strong baselines including LSTM, Transformer, NS Transformer, Informer, Chronos 2, and XGBoost' (6 named) but Table 2 and Section 5 only cover 5
alternatives: XGBoost results may have been run but omitted from Table 2 by editing error
The 'six' in the abstract may itself be a typo for 'five'
implementation_assumptions.affects_section: evaluation_protocol.baselines -- flagged as a paper-internal inconsistency

implementation_assumptions.assumption: Feature counts stated in Section 5.1 ('79, 4, 59, and 7 respectively' for Jane Street/Time-IMM/DSLOB/Optiver in that listed order) do not match the feature counts given in Table 1 (Jane Street 79, Optiver 7, Time-IMM 4, DSLOB 85). Section 5.1 lists '59' where Table 1 says DSLOB has 85 features. This is a direct numeric inconsistency within the paper.
implementation_assumptions.basis: direct textual comparison between Section 5.1 prose and Table 1
alternatives: Table 1's 85 (stated consistently in Sections 3.4 and the DSLOB description) is more likely correct; the '59' in Section 5.1 is likely a transcription error
implementation_assumptions.affects_section: evaluation_protocol.datasets -- flagged as a paper-internal inconsistency; ArXivist uses Table 1's value (85) as authoritative since it is corroborated by Section 3.4's detailed DSLOB description

implementation_assumptions.assumption: Implementation framework not specified; assume PyTorch given the paper's description of 'gradients... backpropagated through the Euler-Maruyama SDE solver via the reparametrisation trick' and 'differentiable convex optimization layers (Agrawal et al. 2019)' (cvxpylayers is a PyTorch/CVXPY-based library), and Hugging Face transformers is explicitly named for loading Chronos-2.
implementation_assumptions.basis: cvxpylayers and Hugging Face transformers are both most commonly used with PyTorch; explicit textual reference to Hugging Face transformers for Chronos-2
alternatives: JAX with a custom differentiable QP solver
TensorFlow (less likely given cvxpylayers/HF ecosystem conventions)

ambiguities.description: Abstract claims 6 baselines including XGBoost; only 5 baselines (LSTM, Transformer, NS-Transformer, Informer, Chronos-2) appear anywhere in the actual results table or baseline description sections.
ambiguities.primary_assumption: XGBoost was either not actually run or its results were omitted from the paper; ArXivist implements only the 5 baselines that are actually described and reported
alternatives: XGBoost results exist but were accidentally dropped from Table 2 during paper preparation

ambiguities.description: Section 5.1 states feature dimensions as '79, 4, 59, and 7' (for Jane Street, Time-IMM, DSLOB, Optiver in that order) while Table 1 and Section 3.4 both state DSLOB has 85 features.
ambiguities.primary_assumption: Table 1's 85 is authoritative (independently corroborated by Section 3.4's detailed feature breakdown: '85 features from four levels'); the '59' in Section 5.1 is a paper-internal error
alternatives: 59 could be correct if Section 5.1 refers to a different/reduced feature subset actually fed to baselines vs. the full 85-feature DSLOB generation described in Section 3.4

ambiguities.description: The paper's own derivation reaches an impasse: penalizing the raw PDE residual to zero would force the market price of risk lambda=0 (i.e., physical and risk-neutral measures coincide, meaning no risk premium exists) which the authors call 'too restrictive.' Their resolution -- training a single auxiliary network V_psi and penalizing its residual -- is explicitly admitted to be 'not sufficient for full no-arbitrage' but merely 'a useful regularizer.'
ambiguities.primary_assumption: Implement L_PDE exactly as operationally described (single auxiliary V_psi, residual under the physical-measure drift/diffusion, MSE penalty) since that is the actual algorithm in Algorithm 1 and Eq. (7), while documenting that the paper itself flags this as theoretically incomplete rather than presenting it as a settled result
alternatives: A more rigorous implementation might use multiple auxiliary V_psi networks ('a family of functions') as the paper's own aside suggests would be more correct
An entirely different no-arbitrage regularization (e.g. explicit risk-neutral drift estimation) could be substituted

ambiguities.description: The main text (Section 4.5) states the total loss as a 3-term sum: L_total = L_forecast + lambda1*L_PDE + lambda2*L_MPR + lambda3*L_consist. Appendix A.8 Eq. (21) gives a 4-term version additionally including lambda4*L_symb. Algorithm 1 (line 21) uses the 3-term version during Phase-1 pretraining and handles L_symb separately in Phase 2 via L_distill (which already includes L_symb per Eq. 14).
ambiguities.primary_assumption: Algorithm 1 is authoritative for the actual training procedure: 3-term L_total during Phase 1, separate L_distill (incorporating L_symb) during Phase 2. Appendix A.8's 4-term formula is a post-hoc unified notation, not a distinct training regime.
alternatives: The symbolic layer's L1 penalty might additionally be applied during Phase 1 pretraining alongside the 3-term loss, contrary to Algorithm 1's explicit 'symbolic layer is not used' statement for Phase 1

ambiguities.location: Table 3 (ablation) DirAcc for A0_Full vs. abstract/main text
ambiguities.description: Table 3 reports A0_Full DirAcc = 0.6489 (64.89%); the abstract and Section 1 state ARTEMIS achieves 64.96% on DSLOB (Table 2's ARTEMIS row). These are two different numbers for what appears to be the same model on the same dataset (0.6496 in Table 2 vs 0.6489 in Table 3's A0_Full row) -- likely due to averaging over different numbers of seeds/runs between the main benchmark and the ablation study, but the paper does not explicitly reconcile this.
ambiguities.primary_assumption: Treat Table 2's 64.96% as the paper's headline reported DSLOB result, and Table 3's 64.89% as a separate ablation-study run of nominally the same full-model configuration; the ~0.07pp difference is within expected seed-to-seed variance and not a substantive discrepancy
alternatives: The two numbers could reflect genuinely different train/eval conditions (e.g. different epoch counts -- Table 3's ablations are explicitly run for only 10 epochs per Figure 6, while Table 2's main benchmark training duration is unspecified)
